skill.name: microservices-patterns
skill.description: Design microservices architectures with service boundaries, event-driven communication, and resilience patterns. Use when building distributed systems, decomposing monoliths, or implementing microservices.
skill.summary: Plan microservice boundaries, communication patterns, distributed data workflows, and resilience controls.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static findings were reviewed against SKILL.md. The external command, weak cryptography, and system reconnaissance detections are false positives from markdown fences or ordinary documentation text. The skill contains HTTP client examples for internal services, which is a low-risk documentation pattern with no automatic execution or exfiltration evidence.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution at markdown code fence lines. These locations delimit Python documentation examples and do not execute commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are visible markdown code fence delimiters around Python examples. No runtime script, shell command, or command interpolation path exists in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged weak cryptography at description and deserializer text. These lines do not contain cryptographic algorithms or security-sensitive crypto decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain words such as description, Designing, and value_deserializer. No evidence found of DES, MD5, RC4, or other weak cryptographic API usage.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detections Are Domain Data Access
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance findings point to customer, transaction, reservation, and order identifiers in microservice examples. They are not host or environment enumeration commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are business identifiers inside illustrative service code. No evidence found of filesystem, process, network interface, or host reconnaissance behavior.

low_findings.title: HTTP Client Examples Require Normal User Review
low_findings.description: The skill includes HTTP client examples and internal service URLs for API gateway and service client patterns. They are documentation snippets with no automatic execution or secret transfer.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network use is explicit and appears legitimate for a microservices skill. Risk remains low because users could adapt the snippets to real services and should review endpoints and data sent.

content.value_statement: Distributed systems fail when service boundaries, communication, and data ownership are unclear. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through practical microservice architecture patterns.
actual_capabilities: Explains service decomposition by business capability and bounded context.
Provides API gateway, REST client, and event-driven communication patterns.
Shows saga orchestration concepts for distributed transaction workflows.
Describes circuit breaker, retry, and bulkhead resilience patterns.
Lists common microservice pitfalls and architecture best practices.
limitations: Does not include runnable project files or production-ready templates in this package.
Does not validate existing infrastructure, runtime traffic, or service ownership automatically.
Example code omits complete imports, dependency setup, and security hardening.
Users must adapt service URLs, authentication, observability, and data policies.

use_cases.title: Plan a Monolith Decomposition
use_cases.description: Map business capabilities to services and identify safe extraction steps.

use_cases.title: Design Service Communication
use_cases.description: Choose between synchronous APIs, event streaming, queues, and gateway aggregation.

use_cases.title: Improve Distributed Resilience
use_cases.description: Add circuit breakers, retries, compensation flows, and failure isolation to service designs.

prompt_templates.title: Identify Service Boundaries
prompt_templates.prompt: Design microservice boundaries for this product domain. List each service, its responsibility, owned data, and main API contracts.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Communication Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these service interactions. Recommend REST, gRPC, messaging, or event streaming for each interaction and explain the tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Add Resilience Controls
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this service dependency graph. Add circuit breakers, retries, timeouts, bulkheads, and fallback behavior where needed.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Saga Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a saga design for this multi-service workflow. Include steps, compensating actions, failure states, and data consistency assumptions.

output_examples.input: Design services for an online store order flow.
output_examples.output: A service boundary proposal covering orders, payments, inventory, and notifications, with owned data and event contracts.
A resilience plan with timeouts, retries, circuit breakers, fallback responses, and compensation behavior for failed payment or inventory steps.

output_examples.input: Decide between REST and events for fulfillment updates.
output_examples.output: A communication recommendation that separates command-style requests from event notifications and explains consistency tradeoffs.

best_practices: Start with clear business capabilities before defining service APIs.
Prefer owned databases and explicit contracts between services.
Design failure handling, tracing, and health checks with every service interaction.
anti_patterns: Splitting services before domain boundaries and ownership are understood.
Sharing databases across services to avoid defining proper contracts.
Using synchronous calls everywhere without timeouts or fallback behavior.

faq.question: Does this skill generate production-ready microservice code?
faq.answer: No. It provides architecture guidance and illustrative examples that require project-specific implementation and review.

faq.question: Can it help decompose a monolith?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers business capability boundaries, bounded contexts, and gradual extraction patterns.

faq.question: Does it support event-driven architecture design?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains event publishing, subscriptions, Kafka-style messaging, and asynchronous workflow tradeoffs.

faq.question: Can it design distributed transactions?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes saga orchestration, compensating actions, and eventual consistency concerns.

faq.question: Does it include security controls?
faq.answer: Only indirectly. Users must add authentication, authorization, secrets handling, and transport security for their environment.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: It is marked for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code in the report metadata.
